title: Bogoliubov mode dynamics and non-adiabatic transitions in time-varying condensed media
abstract: This study investigates non-adiabatic wave dynamics in condensed media and the transition from adiabatic stability to spectral chaos. We introduce a dimensionless parameter, as a universal metric to quantify phase-mode redistribution at sub-wavelength inhomogeneities. Our framework treats defects as localized sites of adiabaticity violation triggering non-adiabatic parametric excitation of the ground state. Numerical validation in an expanded 50-level bosonic basis demonstrates that the framework accurately distinguishes between adiabatic regimes in ENZ-metamaterials and non-adiabatic transitions in ultrafast magnetic media . We establish a universal scaling law governed by the non-adiabaticity-to-regulation ratio, proving that the proposed metric remains a robust metrological tool for identifying sub-wavelength inhomogeneities across diverse material classes. Computational singularities observed at extreme loads identify the rigorous operational boundaries for coherent mode-mixing. The robustness of the proposed framework is numerically validated, proving the method's reliability for a wide class of non-linear condensed media satisfying the stability criterion. This result provides a rigorous physical justification for the dynamic Hilbert space truncation (effective fermion-like dynamics), ensuring metrological consistency in complex structural environments. These results provide a theoretical foundation for probing ultrafast collective excitations and latent internal stresses, extending structural analysis beyond the traditional diffraction barrier.
summary: This paper proposes a new method for non-destructive testing of materials by measuring the production of phase-conjugate Bogoliubov modes during rapid parameter changes. By using a new dimensionless metric, the author demonstrates that sub-wavelength defects can be detected through phase dynamics even when they are invisible to traditional amplitude-based methods.
main_problem: Developing a metrological framework to detect sub-wavelength structural defects in time-varying condensed media by analyzing non-adiabatic phase-mode redistribution.
main_result: The establishment of a universal dimensionless non-adiabaticity parameter (eta) and a scaling law (xi = eta/U) that allows for the detection of defects below the Rayleigh diffraction limit.
method: Numerical simulations using the QuTiP engine with a large-scale bosonic Fock basis and Lindblad Master Equation approach, validated by high-precision RK4 integration.
model_or_system: Time-varying condensed media such as ultrafast magnetic media, ENZ metamaterials, and magnetoelectric composites, modeled by a second-order oscillator equation with time-dependent frequency and Kerr-type anharmonicity.
key_observables: Bogoliubov coefficients (u and v), phase-mode redistribution, spectral asymmetry ratio (|v|^2/|u|^2), and Fock-state occupancy.
important_parameters: Non-adiabaticity parameter (eta), stability ratio (xi = eta/U), anharmonicity (U), and critical spatial scale (L_crit).
main_assumptions: The use of a truncated Hilbert space is valid if high-level Fock state occupancy is negligible; the system follows a Euclidean normalization (|u|^2 + |v|^2 approx 1) due to dissipation.
figures_summary: Figures show Fock-state occupancy, high-resolution phase-coherence, and thermodynamic audits of the stability map.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of sub-wavelength defect detection, defines the non-adiabaticity metric, classifies material regimes (adiabatic vs. non-adiabatic), provides numerical validation of the scaling law, and concludes with the robustness of the framework against noise.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous framework for studying non-equilibrium dynamics, mode-mixing, and the breakdown of the WKB approximation in driven-dissipative bosonic systems, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
